message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>To battle the COVID-19 pandemic, widespread testing for the presence of the SARS-CoV-2 virus is worldwide being employed by specific real-time RT-PCR (rRT-PCR) of viral RNA. The CDC has issued a recommended panel of PCR-based test sets that entail several primer/probe sets that target the SARS-CoV-2 N-gene, but also one that targets the human RNase P gene (h-RP) as a positive control for RNA extraction and/or reverse-transcription (RT) efficacy.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>We discovered that the CDC-recommended h-RP primer/probe set has a faulty design, because both PCR primers are located in the same exon, which allows for unwanted PCR-amplification of background genomic DNA (gDNA). By removing RNA from nose-swab samples by an RNase treatment, we showed that the presence of gDNA in samples resulted in false-positive signals for the h-RP test control. This is rather serious, because it could lead to false-negative test outcomes, since the CDC interpretation of an absent SARS-CoV-2 rRT-PCR signal plus a positive h-RP rRT-PCR signal is interpreted as “2019-nCoV not detected”, whereas a false-positive h-RP rRT-PCR signal resulting from amplification of gDNA should be interpreted as “Invalid Result” and the procedure should be repeated.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>In order to overhaul the faulty h-RP rRT-PCR primer/probe set with minimal modification, we designed and tested several new h-RP reverse primers. Replacement of the CDC-recommended PCR reverse primer with our selected exon-exon junction reverse primer corrected the problem of false-positive results with this important SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR test control and thus eliminated the problem of potential false-negative COVID-19 diagnoses.</jats:p>
title: Overhauling a faulty control in the CDC-recommended SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR test panel

reference.article-title: RT-qPCR Testing of SARS-CoV-2: A Primer

reference.unstructured: Laboratory testing strategy recommendations for COVID-19, 21 March 2020. WHO refno: WHO/2019-nCoV/lab_testing/2020.1

reference.article-title: Pitfalls of Quantitative Real-Time Reverse-Transcription Polymerase Chain Reaction

reference.unstructured: Rosebrock AP , Patient DNA cross-reactivity of the CDC SARS-CoV-2 extraction control leads to an inherent potential for false negative results. BioRxiv https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.05.13.094839
